original_abstract:
This article explores the missional potential of Ezra-Nehemiah (EN) by examining how the written Word of God functions as a catalyst for societal transformation. By employing historical, canonical, and exegetical methods, the study argues that in the postexilic context of Judah, spiritual renewal mediated through the sacred text was a necessary prerequisite for social reconstruction. The analysis situates EN within the tension between oral and literate cultures, highlighting how the "book" of the Law of Moses exerted authority over the returned community and dictated the terms of their religious and social identity.

The research further traces this movement into the New Testament, drawing parallels between the postexilic community’s struggle for theological integrity and the early church’s mission within the Roman Empire. Special attention is given to the controversial issues of separation and syncretism, suggesting that the preservation of a distinct theological identity is essential for missional effectiveness. The article concludes by offering missiological guidelines for a contemporary theology of mission, emphasizing that the church serves as the primary agent through which the transformative power of Scripture impacts the broader world.
humanized_abstract:
Mission in Ezra-Nehemiah The project aims to investigate the missional potential of Ezra-Nehemiah (EN). The study aims to evaluate the role of the written Word of God as an agent of social change within the context of post-exilic Judah. It will employ historical, canonical and exegetical methods to examine whether the spiritual restoration brought about through the written Word of God was a condition precedent for the rebuilding of society. The study situates EN within the context of the struggle between oral and literate cultures, with particular reference to the role of the “book” of the Law of Moses in EN, which imposed rules and regulations upon the restored community of Judah, thereby determining their religious and social existence.

The study pursues this line of inquiry into the New Testament and bridges the postexilic history of the biblical period with the New Testament era in order to illuminate common issues and challenges facing God’s people in each respective era. Special attention is given to the perennial contemporary issues of separation and syncretism, particularly from the perspective of maintaining the integrity of the doctrine to which the mission exists to promote. The study seeks to derive missiological lessons for contemporary theology of mission, and to reiterate that the church is God’s ordained vehicle whereby Scripture can transform the world in which we live.
